articles.title: STAT+: Trump’s health care affordability czar takes aim at hospitals
articles.description: Plus a buried alcohol study gets published, and a Biosecure update.

articles.title: Sam Altman’s Eyeball Scanning Company Now Laying Off Workers

articles.title: Meet Astronaut Andre Douglas — ready to bring more moon joy — on Artemis III
articles.description: NASA announced the Artemis III crew on Tuesday. NPR's Scott Detrow speaks with Andre Douglas who will serve as a mission specialist.

articles.title: NASA names 4 astronauts on the 'highly complex' Artemis III lunar training mission
articles.description: The crew of four — NASA astronaut and commander Randy Bresnik, European Space Agency pilot Luca Parmitano, NASA mission specialists Frank Rubio and Andre Douglas — are scheduled to launch next year.

articles.title: Firefighters face a higher risk of skin cancer, but nano fabrics with tiny, rough fibers can help keep them safer
articles.description: Wildland firefighters are exposed to a mix of harmful chemicals in the smoke they breathe and the ash and soot that gets on their clothing. Over long assignments fighting fires that can last for days to weeks, those chemicals can be absorbed by their skin.

articles.title: Dino-killing asteroid may have fueled underground life for 8 million years
articles.description: The asteroid that caused the extinction of the dinosaurs also created an underground environment suited to supporting new life, and new research suggests it lasted for millions of years longer than previously suspected.

articles.title: The US cities where the housing market runs on generational wealth
articles.description: A study published by the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) analyzed US Census Bureau, property, and income tax records for more than 3.4 million families.

articles.title: Why Can't the Universe Be Cyclic? Part 4: When a Good Idea Meets Bad Data
articles.description: The ekpyrotic universe is a beautiful idea that runs headlong into the data. From hand-waved singularities and assumed dark energy to the killer blow from Planck and WMAP measurements of the cosmic microwave background, here is why nature has so far voted against it.

articles.title: How animals use leveling behaviors to put alphas in their place
articles.description: Inequality is not unique to human groups and societies. Individuals with relatively little power possess a variety of behavioral strategies to counterbalance or regulate power differences. In humans, these strategies include criticism, ridicule, disobedience, or even the expulsion or execution of powerful individuals. Similar inequality-reducing behaviors, which carry comparable social costs, are also found in animal societies.

articles.title: Investigators Questioning Bill Gates Over Ties to Jeffrey Epstein
articles.description: What a mess. The post Investigators Questioning Bill Gates Over Ties to Jeffrey Epstein appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: Insular cortex linked to brain stimulation’s antidepressant effects
articles.description: A circuit that runs from the prefrontal cortex to the insular cortex appears to mediate the antidepressant effects of a newer form of transcranial magnetic stimulation, according to a study led by Weill Cornell Medicine investigators.

articles.title: An expert explains: The tricks Messi and Ronaldo use to delay body aging
articles.description: From explosive power to recovery management: How the two superstars manage to maintain their bodies even in the fifth decade of life, and what all of us can adopt from them.

articles.title: Astronauts capture rare 'dancing' aurora caused by powerful solar eruption
articles.description: NASA astronaut Jessica Meir filmed the time lapse of the Southern Lights over Antartica from onboard the SpaceX Dragon spacecraft.

articles.title: World’s slowest experiment in Australia has been running for nearly 100 years
articles.description: The Pitch Drop Experiment began in 1927 to show that pitch, a tar-like material that appears solid.

articles.description: Penn State researchers have built a human-eye-inspired device to prevent self-driving cars from going blind in changing light conditions.

articles.title: 'Tokenomics is beginning': The firm behind the viral AI doomsday post says rising costs will reshape the market's hottest trade
articles.description: Citrini Research predicts that the economics of tokenmaxxing will prompt a shift to "edge AI" that will have implications for investors.

articles.description: By Will Dunham WASHINGTON, June 9 (Reuters) - About 120 million years ago in what is now northwestern China, many kinds of birds flocked to a lakeside ecosys...

articles.title: NASA Will Announce New Artemis III Astronauts and an Update on Its Moon Program
articles.description: The agency will announce the crew of Artemis III on Tuesday. But will the mission be ready to fly in 2027?

articles.title: The asteroid that killed the dinosaurs may have sparked millions of years of hydrothermal life
articles.description: When asteroids slam into Earth, they can create hydrothermal vent systems

articles.title: Doctors turn to secret AI tools as hospitals fail to keep up with technology, survey finds
articles.description: The study, Philips Future Health Index, was carried out through two quantitative surveys, one with 2,011 healthcare professionals and another with 20,085 patients across 10 countries

articles.title: Sloth genes could unlock secrets to healthy ageing and space travel, study finds
articles.description: Scientists believe the study could help inform future research on tissue preservation, critical care medicine, ageing and metabolic disease

articles.title: Opinion: Ending animal testing could set back xenotransplantation just as the field is poised for a breakthrough
articles.description: “Innovations like xenotransplantation would never have reached clinical trials without animal experimentation,” writes transplant surgeon Joshua Mezrich.

articles.title: The prescription that became a daily habit: The medication you probably do not need
articles.description: Millions of people worldwide take heartburn medications, often continuing them for years without stopping to ask whether the treatment is still necessary.

articles.title: New positive data for Viaskin Peanut (DBV Technologies)
articles.description: DBV Technologies has reported new positive data from its Phase 3 VITESSE study evaluating the Viaskin Peanut patch in children aged 4 to 7 with peanut allergies, alongside an overview of the design...

articles.title: Onions bring tears to your eyes – but it is time to expose their truth
articles.description: Dr. Maya Rosman's aunt's nostalgic onion pie alongside all the scientific truth about the virtues of onions and the ways to defeat the tears.

articles.title: MLB swing-tracking data helps researchers examine baseball's long-debated two-strike approach
articles.description: When baseball fans watch a batter strike out with runners in scoring position, the reaction is often immediate: Shorten the swing. Put the ball in play. Stop swinging for the fences, they lament.

articles.title: Expedition to Antarctica advances research on potential melanoma treatment
articles.description: Deep beneath the icy waters surrounding Antarctica, a small marine organism may hold clues to a future cancer treatment. Researchers from USF recently returned from a six-week expedition in one of the most remote environments on Earth to study a species of ascidian, or sea squirt, that contains a bacterium capable of killing melanoma cancer cells.

articles.title: Planet nine mystery deepens as new discovery challenges hidden planet theory
articles.description: Astronomers have spent years searching for a possible hidden giant planet far beyond Neptune. Unusual orbits among distant Kuiper Belt objects have fueled the Planet Nine theory, but recent discoveries are challenging the idea by showing more stable motion than expected. If Planet Nine exists, it may be much farther away than originally thought.

articles.title: Master’s grad builds an AI-powered bridge for neurodivergent students | Folio
articles.description: Francerlândio Macena da Cruz drew on his own experiences to create an app that helps learners overcome overthinking.

articles.title: How plants survive constant DNA damage: Newly identified repair protein protects growth-critical stem cells
articles.description: Similar to the way DNA damage can contribute to human diseases such as cancer, it can also disrupt growth, development and survival in plants. Every day, plants endure environmental stresses such as sunlight, radiation, drought and soil stress—all of which can damage their DNA. However, they cannot move away from danger. How do plants handle all that damage?

articles.title: Ring founder responds to lawsuit over A.I. and privacy concerns
Scientists found a new Alzheimer’s trigger and a drug that stops it
articles.description: Researchers have identified a new Alzheimer’s target and created an experimental compound that blocks a damaging process inside brain cells. In mice, the treatment slowed nerve cell loss, reduced Alzheimer’s-related changes, and even appeared to promote healthier aging.

articles.title: Peptide blocks DNA breaks tied to treatment-induced leukemia, offering new prevention route
articles.description: Thanks to effective therapies, more and more people are now able to live with or after cancer in the long term. Consequently, the number of patients affected by the long-term effects of their treatment is also increasing. Secondary leukemias are particularly serious. These can develop when cellular stress caused by chemotherapy or radiotherapy triggers DNA breaks in specific regions of the genome. If these breaks are incorrectly repaired by the body's own repair mechanisms, detrimental rearrangements can occur that promote the development of leukemia.

articles.title: Tiny wearable sensor aims to improve heart health monitoring from patients’ own home
articles.description: UNSW researchers have developed a lightweight wearable sensor that could potentially continuously check on people with heart and breathing problems to help doctors identify problems more quickly.

articles.title: Built by nature, reimagined by engineers
articles.description: UNSW Canberra researchers are translating insect design into smarter, more adaptable technologies for the real world.

articles.title: University of Waterloo researchers develop lightweight X-ray aprons to minimize chronic pain
articles.description: Researchers at the University of Waterloo have developed a lightweight alternative to heavy lead aprons worn by X-ray technologists.

articles.title: New AI-Designed Universal Vaccine — What It Could Mean for Future Pandemics Before They Begin
articles.description: Learn about a new vaccine technology that uses a “super-antigen” to protect against thousands of virus variants, from SARS-CoV-2 to Ebola, and beyond.

articles.title: Bumblebees can solve complex puzzles like chimpanzees and elephants, study finds
articles.description: Researchers say a new study proves that insects can solve problems spontaneously, a cognitive ability usually associated with large-brained vertebrates.

articles.title: Hidden geometry explains why kernel methods separate complex data so well
articles.description: Are two sets of data genuinely different, or is it because of randomness? This question, known as the two-sample testing problem, becomes notoriously difficult in modern datasets, because they are often high-dimensional, complex, and differences between them can take countless subtle forms.

articles.title: 'From STEM to earn': High school programs aimed at diversifying the field drive gains in college, salaries
articles.description: High school students participating in pipeline programs aimed at increasing diversity in STEM fields are more likely to enroll in—and graduate from—elite colleges with a related degree. In addition, such improvements raised their predicted earnings by anywhere from 3% to 15%, according to a study co-authored by a University of Michigan researcher published in the Journal of Human Resources.

articles.title: Why this $10 spectrometer chip could bring real-time chemical sensing to wearables
articles.description: Researchers from the University of Cambridge and GlitterinTech, a startup founded by the same research group, have unveiled a fundamentally new type of optical spectrometer that delivers laboratory-grade precision in a device small enough to be embedded in portable and wearable technologies. By rethinking how spectra are measured and processed, the team has demonstrated a spectrometer costing only around $10, operating at a centimeter scale, and capable of applications ranging from industrial quality control to real-time health care monitoring.

articles.title: Even Light Drinking Linked to an Increased Risk of Several Types of Cancer — What You Need to Know
articles.description: Learn how no level of alcohol intake appears safe for cancer, while low-to-moderate drinking shows mixed effects on cardiometabolic and neurological conditions.

articles.title: If You Think AI Companies Are Unethical Now, Wait Until They Go Public
articles.description: It's going to get messy. The post If You Think AI Companies Are Unethical Now, Wait Until They Go Public appeared first on Futurism .
